content:
# Ruth Chapter 4 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 882 | **Source Image:** RUT_004_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Now Boaz went up to the gate and sat down there. And behold, the kinsman-redeemer of whom Boaz had spoken was passing by. And he said, "Turn aside, sit down here, friend." And he turned aside and sat down.

**2** And he took ten men of the elders of the city and said, "Sit down here." And they sat down.

**3** And he said to the kinsman-redeemer, "The portion of the field that belonged to our brother Elimelech -- Naomi, who has returned from the fields of Moab, is selling it.

**4** And I said, 'I will disclose it to your ear, saying: Acquire it before those sitting here and before the elders of my people. If you will redeem it, redeem. But if you will not redeem, tell me, that I may know, for there is no one besides you to redeem, and I am after you.'" And he said, "I will redeem it."

**5** And Boaz said, "On the day you acquire the field from the hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of the dead, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance."

**6** And the kinsman-redeemer said, "I am not able to redeem it for myself, lest I impair my own inheritance. Redeem for yourself what I would redeem, for I am not able to redeem it."

**7** Now this was the custom formerly in Israel concerning redemption and concerning exchange, to confirm any matter: a man would draw off his sandal and give it to his neighbor. And this was the manner of attestation in Israel.

**8** And the kinsman-redeemer said to Boaz, "Acquire it for yourself." And he drew off his sandal.

**9** And Boaz said to the elders and to all the people, "You are witnesses this day that I have acquired all that belonged to Elimelech, and all that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon, from the hand of Naomi.

**10** And also Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, I have acquired as my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his place. You are witnesses this day."

**11** And all the people who were in the gate, and the elders, said, "We are witnesses. May the LORD make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and like Leah, the two who built the house of Israel. May you act worthily in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem.

**12** And may your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, through the offspring which the LORD will give you from this young woman."

**13** So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. And he went in to her, and the LORD gave her conception, and she bore a son.

**14** And the women said to Naomi, "Blessed be the LORD, who has not left you this day without a kinsman-redeemer. And may his name be renowned in Israel.

**15** He shall be to you a restorer of life and a sustainer of your old age, for your daughter-in-law, who loves you, has borne him -- she who is better to you than seven sons."

**16** And Naomi took the child and laid him on her bosom, and she became his nurse.

**17** And the neighbor women gave him a name, saying, "A son has been born to Naomi." And they called his name Obed. He is the father of Jesse, the father of David.

**18** Now these are the generations of Perez: Perez begot Hezron,

**19** and Hezron begot Ram, and Ram begot Amminadab,

**20** and Amminadab begot Nahshon, and Nahshon begot Salmon,

**21** and Salmon begot Boaz, and Boaz begot Obed,

**22** and Obed begot Jesse, and Jesse begot David.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 4 is the legal and genealogical climax of the book. The gate scene (vv. 1-12) is a formal judicial proceeding, and SOPHIA has maintained elevated, formal register throughout to match the solemnity of the legal transaction.

Manuscript verification: The Leningrad Codex image (page 882) confirms the text of Chapter 4. The final page shows Ruth's conclusion and the genealogical appendix. The Masoretic colophon and verse-counting notes are visible at the bottom of the text, marking the end of the scroll.

Verses where all three substantially agreed: 2, 7, 8, 9, 13, 17 (core naming scene), 18-22 (genealogy). The genealogy required minimal arbitration as all three follow the same names and sequence, differing only in whether to use "begot" (EMET/LOGOS) or "fathered" (PNEUMA).

Key term decisions for this chapter:
- **go'el** (throughout): "Kinsman-redeemer" -- maintained consistently.
- **peloni almoni** (4:1): See disagreement log -- rendered "friend" as a form of address.
- **eshet hayil** echo: Ruth is now fully established as Boaz's counterpart through the gate scene.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 -- peloni almoni: "so-and-so" vs. "friend"
- **EMET:** "'Turn-aside, sit-down here, so-and-so' [peloni almoni]"
- **LOGOS:** "'Turn aside, sit down here, So-and-so'"
- **PNEUMA:** "'Come over here, friend -- sit down'"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts a hybrid: "Turn aside, sit down here, friend." The Hebrew peloni almoni is an idiom meaning "so-and-so" or "a certain unnamed person" -- it is the narrator's way of withholding the name, not necessarily what Boaz actually called the man. EMET and LOGOS render the idiom literally, which reads strangely as direct address in English. PNEUMA's "friend" provides a plausible form of address while preserving the anonymity. However, SOPHIA retains EMET/LOGOS's "Turn aside" over PNEUMA's "Come over here," as the Hebrew surah is specifically "turn aside [from your path]."

### Verse 3 -- "has sold" vs. "is selling"
- **EMET:** "has-sold Naomi" (Hebrew perfect tense)
- **LOGOS:** "Naomi... is selling it" (present progressive)
- **PNEUMA:** "Naomi... is selling the piece of land"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "is selling." While EMET correctly notes the Hebrew perfect tense (makherah), the context of a legal proceeding suggests a present or imminent sale -- Boaz is bringing the matter to the gate for adjudication. LOGOS's "is selling" fits the legal context better. EMET's own note acknowledges the ambiguity.

### Verse 3 -- "our brother" vs. "our relative"
- **EMET:** "to-our-brother, to-Elimelech"
- **LOGOS:** "our brother Elimelech"
- **PNEUMA:** "our relative Elimelech"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "our brother Elimelech." The Hebrew ahinu ("our brother") is a kinship term used in legal proceedings for a fellow clansman. PNEUMA's "relative" is weaker and loses the legal convention.

### Verse 4 -- "I will uncover your ear" vs. "I will disclose it to your ear" vs. "bring this to your attention"
- **EMET:** "I-will-uncover your-ear"
- **LOGOS:** "I will disclose it to your ear"
- **PNEUMA:** "I thought I should bring this to your attention"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "I will disclose it to your ear." The Hebrew egleh oznekha ("I will uncover your ear") is an idiom for disclosure. LOGOS preserves the body-part idiom while making it comprehensible. EMET's literal rendering sounds bizarre in English. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the idiom entirely.

### Verse 4 -- "Buy" vs. "Acquire" vs. "Buy"
- **EMET:** "Buy [it]"
- **LOGOS:** "Acquire it"
- **PNEUMA:** "Buy it"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "Acquire" -- the Hebrew qeneh carries the broader sense of acquisition (including legal transfer), not merely commercial purchase. "Acquire" is the more precise legal term.

### Verse 5 -- "Moabitess" vs. "Moabite" and "wife of the dead" vs. "widow of the dead man"
- **EMET:** "Ruth the-Moabitess, [the]-wife of-the-dead, you-buy [it]"
- **LOGOS:** "Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of the dead"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead man"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Moabitess" is consistent with prior chapters. "Wife of the dead" preserves the Hebrew eshet hammet literally -- the stark phrase "wife of the dead" is a legal designation, not merely "widow." PNEUMA's softening to "widow of the dead man" weakens the legal formula.

### Verse 6 -- "ruin" vs. "impair" vs. "jeopardize"
- **EMET:** "lest I-ruin my-[own]-inheritance"
- **LOGOS:** "lest I impair my own inheritance"
- **PNEUMA:** "it would jeopardize my own inheritance"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "impair." The Hebrew ashkhit means "destroy/ruin/corrupt." "Impair" captures the sense of damaging the inheritance without the total destruction implied by EMET's "ruin" or the risk-language of PNEUMA's "jeopardize."

### Verse 10 -- "from with his brothers" vs. "from among his brothers"
- **EMET:** "from-with his-brothers"
- **LOGOS:** "from among his brothers"
- **PNEUMA:** "from among his relatives"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "From among" is natural English for the Hebrew me'im. PNEUMA changes "brothers" (ahav) to "relatives," which is interpretive.

### Verse 10 -- "from the gate of his place" vs. "from the records of his town"
- **EMET:** "from-[the]-gate of-his-place"
- **LOGOS:** "from the gate of his place"
- **PNEUMA:** "from the records of his town"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "from the gate of his place." The Hebrew misha'ar meqomo refers to the civic assembly at the gate, the legal center. PNEUMA's "records of his town" is an interpretive modernization.

### Verse 11 -- "do valiantly" vs. "act worthily" vs. "prosper"
- **EMET:** "And-do valiantly in-Ephrathah, and-call [a]-name in-Beth-lehem"
- **LOGOS:** "May you act worthily in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem"
- **PNEUMA:** "May you prosper in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "act worthily." The Hebrew 'aseh hayil means "do/make valor/worth" -- the same root hayil again. "Act worthily" preserves the hayil connection. PNEUMA's "prosper" narrows the meaning to material success. EMET's "do valiantly" is close but sounds archaic.

### Verse 11 -- "call a name" vs. "be renowned"
- **EMET:** "call [a]-name in-Beth-lehem"
- **LOGOS:** "be renowned in Bethlehem"
- **PNEUMA:** "be renowned in Bethlehem"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Hebrew qera' shem means literally "call a name" but idiomatically means "be famous/renowned." The idiomatic rendering is correct here.

### Verse 12 -- "from the seed" vs. "through the offspring"
- **EMET:** "from the-seed which YHWH will-give to-you"
- **LOGOS:** "through the offspring which the LORD will give you"
- **PNEUMA:** "through the offspring the LORD gives you"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Offspring" is the standard rendering of zera' in formal translation. PNEUMA changes the tense from future to present.

### Verse 13 -- "he went in to her" vs. "He slept with her"
- **EMET:** "he-went-in to-her"
- **LOGOS:** "he went in to her"
- **PNEUMA:** "He slept with her"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "he went in to her" -- the Hebrew vayyavo' eleiha is the standard biblical euphemism for conjugal relations. "He went in to her" preserves the idiom. PNEUMA's "slept with" is a different (modern) euphemism that loses the Hebrew coloring.

### Verse 14 -- go'el: "kinsman-redeemer" vs. "redeemer"
- **EMET:** "a-kinsman-redeemer [go'el]"
- **LOGOS:** "a kinsman-redeemer"
- **PNEUMA:** "a redeemer"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. The full compound "kinsman-redeemer" is maintained for consistency. PNEUMA's bare "redeemer" loses the kinship dimension.

### Verse 14 -- "may his name be called in Israel" vs. "may his name be renowned in Israel" vs. "May this child become famous throughout Israel"
- **EMET:** "may-his-name be-called in-Israel"
- **LOGOS:** "may his name be renowned in Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "May this child become famous throughout Israel"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "may his name be renowned in Israel." EMET's "be called" is too literal for yiqqare'; "be renowned" captures the sense of fame. PNEUMA's "this child become famous throughout Israel" is a significant paraphrase.

### Verse 15 -- "restorer of life" vs. "He will renew your life"
- **EMET:** "for-a-restorer of-life, and-to-sustain your-old-age"
- **LOGOS:** "a restorer of life and a sustainer of your old age"
- **PNEUMA:** "He will renew your life and sustain you in your old age"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew meshiv nefesh ("restorer of soul/life") is a noun phrase; LOGOS preserves this. PNEUMA converts to a verb clause.

### Verse 15 -- "better to you than seven sons" vs. "worth more to you than seven sons"
- **EMET:** "who she [is] better to-you than-seven sons"
- **LOGOS:** "she who is better to you than seven sons"
- **PNEUMA:** "she who is worth more to you than seven sons"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "better to you than seven sons." The Hebrew tovah lakh ("good/better to you") is rendered most directly as "better." PNEUMA's "worth more" introduces economic language not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 16 -- "placed him in her bosom" vs. "laid him on her bosom" vs. "cradled him in her arms"
- **EMET:** "she-placed-him in-her-bosom"
- **LOGOS:** "laid him on her bosom"
- **PNEUMA:** "cradled him in her arms"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "laid him on her bosom." The Hebrew beheyqah means "in/on her bosom/lap." PNEUMA's "cradled him in her arms" is a modern interpretive rendering.

### Verse 16 -- "nurse" vs. "guardian"
- **EMET:** "she-was to-him for-a-nurse"
- **LOGOS:** "she became his guardian"
- **PNEUMA:** "became his nurse"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA with "nurse." The Hebrew omenet means "nurse" or "foster-mother" -- one who carries and tends a child. LOGOS's "guardian" shifts toward a legal concept not implied by the Hebrew term.

### Verses 18-22 -- "begot" vs. "fathered"
- **EMET:** "begot"
- **LOGOS:** "begot"
- **PNEUMA:** "fathered"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "begot." The Hebrew holid is the hiphil of yalad ("to bear/beget"). "Begot" is the traditional genealogical term and preserves the formal register of the toledot formula. PNEUMA's "fathered" is correct but informal.

### Verse 20 -- Salmah/Salmon spelling
- **EMET:** "Salmah" (v. 20) / "Salmon" (v. 21) -- preserves Codex variant
- **LOGOS:** "Salmon" (both verses)
- **PNEUMA:** "Salmon" (both verses)
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "Salmon" in both verses for consistency and readability, while noting that the Leningrad Codex preserves the variant spelling Salmah in v. 20.

---

*Signed, SOPHIA*
